speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 3. Irene, you were telling me that the Russians and the Germans were exchanging Polish prisoners of war. What happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was exchanged when I was saying I was kept by the Russian. And now [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went all the way to the-- to the railroad and fell asleep. Early in the morning, there was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] movement. People were there in bunches with children, with-- their bundles were coming out. And there was a German officer and Russian. Well, naturally, I went to the German and-- because I just escaped. And I didn't have that-- my little, little piece of paper. I hide it in my bosom.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that paper for?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was that I can leave, you know. Because they arrested me. When I did have already-- and then my transport left. So I took-- I am taking [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: So I go to the officer and say, Herr Lieutenant, I was supposed to leave last night. But I was not feeling well. I did have a fever. And I don't have any luggage, you know. I am alone. Could you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I handed him the paper. He looked at the paper. He said, this transport is leaving today. It was a miracle. There was some partisans-- something did to the transport that they did not ship.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was in a train at-- it was about 4 o' clock in the morning. I was on my knees and praying. I am living.

speaker: interviewee
text: We thought that we'd go, that I'd go to Radom right away. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when the train left, maybe, for two hours or so, or three hours, they disconnected three wagons. And they put us into a special place.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is-- what you call it? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Checking out and doing the askings and this-- Interrogating?

speaker: interviewee
text: Interrogating. And also, this is that we don't bring any sicknesses-- quarantine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Quarantine.

speaker: interviewee
text: I could not remember the word. So we did-- they took us in the-- they were German now. And they told us that we will go to our homes if we tell the truth-- and we have to-- this. So they give us big speech. And we were surrounded all around. We could not go out. And there, I realized now there were many women and men. There were many women that were crying with little children. I find out that the men were taken away-- Jewish men. I find out, at that point, talking with the others that the, the German-- whoever was Jewish man was circumcised. They took it away. We were living, you know, three, four ups, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in a camp, like--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, quarantined. There is a camp. First thing when we walked in, men were standing. Take off the clothes. Go to the showers. The Germans are very clean murderers. And so they sprayed something in our hair. They spray our clothing that was smelling unbelievable. And then they say we will be there for three weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Irene, let's go back for a moment to Russia. When you left Svetlana, you came to the railroad station. And you were arrested.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened while you were arrested in Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a-- I mean, I was at-- arrested. That means I arrested by the Russian because Dr. Kensov said that I am German spy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they tested. They look for my papers. And they-- and when they came to my breast, I got red in my face. So the commissar was laughing, you know, and this. So they kept me for two days, trying to find out-- he thought that I am really a spy or something, some partisan organization or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he thought that before Dr. Kensov come from Moscow that he will be able to get from the threat to the whole thing. And I disappointed him. Because when he finally allowed me to go to my girlfriend-- that I just made up that false name-- and went to the different fences. And I came-- I escaped. Did, did they abuse you while you were a prisoner?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well physically, yes, because they didn't give me food. And they woke me up every five minutes and asking all the questions. Finally, the commissar said to me-- said, I'm sorry for you. Because I don't know. I did not have a gun. I am not a-- not a, a, a spy. I say, Kensov tried to rape me. And I was scared, you know, because it happened to me in a forest right in the beginning. I told him, though. So he said, OK, I am taking on my conscience-- I let you go. But I sent you with somebody.

speaker: interviewee
text: So now I am in the train. And they disconnected the train. It was my luck that the transport-- a miracle that the transport that was supposed to leave the, the night I was arrested did not leave-- leaving now. So I did not have the problem with the German.

speaker: interviewee
text: He just-- OK. And I went into the train. Now I am there and seeing all kinds of horrible things. I become very sick. I did have a very bad flu.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the quarantine?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the quarantine. That's the reason they put me into special place, so I am not with the other. And I could not believe it. Three weeks now is coming. And they say we can go, OK? I was first in that train. And I am-- we, we were packed like herrings there. And traveling-- and I did see the destructions of the war now. Because, I mean, I was going that way now. I go back. I see the destructions, I see the bombarding houses, and this. We come to Radom. I am in-- dressed in a little shoes that are falling off my feet and in a man's military coat. I come there. And it was still cool, you know. And I cried. And I cried because I don't know-- nobody's waiting for me. And I know that I have a end there. But I went so much through that I didn't-- I forgot the name of my aunt. I forgot where she lives. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Man with the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, with the horse and buggy, did see me crying, saying that-- he said, what you crying, child? I said, well, I just came from the Russian side. I don't know. I cannot find my aunt. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, well, wait minute. The people that I will take care will come. You can sit with me. And I drop them off, and then tell me where.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember the field that was all [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] plain and that the bumps were falling. And I did remember that somehow was close to my aunt's house. So I more or less explained to him. So there he comes, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I did see my aunt's house, a white little fence. And I said, that's my-- that's my aunt's house. So he say, I will wait till, till you go in. So I open the gate. A beautiful young girl with the black hair came towards me. That was [? Bronya. ?] One look at-- I did not recognize her. One look at her and I was screaming, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was screaming, Irene, Irene, Irene! But instead of going to me, she was running-- she was running to the house. So I am now-- my mother, my father, my sister, everybody start coming. My father, in the meantime, escaped. Because when the German took over, we were-- though we had a German name, but my father is Wladislaw Gut. And he was a very proud Pole.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they put a band on him with the letter P, Pole. He took whatever valuable was and left and came to my aunt's house. And meantime, I was in Russia already. So the house was very small. They lived on a attic. My father was an engineer and chemist and-- architect and chemist. He tried to help to keep my mother and my sisters so that they have a food.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't-- they did not have furniture or anything. So he was doing from the tires of the car, they make it sewed from the little boots my mother was sewing. And they were selling that-- to try to. My father was a handsome man in '39. When I did see him now, his cheeks were falling in. The shirt-- you can put a hand in it. And my mother was gray, completely. And I could not-- in such a short time to see the-- what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: That now is 1942. And-- [? Bronya ?] was how old when you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she was a little girl. She did have also surgery. That's what I didn't recognize her, too. Because her hair changed completely.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she recognized you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She recognized me. But she was so excited that she ran home instead of coming greeting me. So now it is May, my birthday. And that was the most wonderful birthday. My father was there. We went to, to little park-- and reminiscing all the little things that we did before the war. And I was telling my father that I was raped. I told him. I remember he said, Irene, don't let that-- not ruin your life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] God has-- you survived. God has other plans for you, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] something important for you. And it was a wonderful, wonderful time. But now the time come, I came-- I tried to find work. Everyone-- my sister and this because we-- the winter will come. We could not stay in the attic, you know. We didn't have anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my-- Janina, my second sister, she took a job in the 'Mama Papa' little store. And we tried to-- I come. I was looking for a job. I come home. My mother was crying, and my sister crying.

speaker: interviewee
text: They came and took my father away-- the Germans because he builded the factory in Kozlowa Gora. He changed the machinery. The Germans could not work on this. And they need it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they were looking for him. So they came and took my father away. They find him. They did not take my mother or sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now the-- September is coming. That's my mother's birthday. And she surprised us. She said that she's going to my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kozlowa Gora. Our home was taken away because we did have a nice villa, furniture. The Germans took it away. So she-- they left him to live in a little. And they put the band on him, Pole, you know. And so mother-- father is alone. And he was spoiled because we were five girls. My grandfather and mother, even the dog and cat were females, too. He was one male in our family.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother say, I am going because we have not-- we will not survive. We don't have home. It's cold. We will not survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: We pleaded with her. She didn't want to take me or my sister because we were pretty young girls. She say, when I take you, they take you to the pleasure homes. Because that's what they did with pretty, blond, nice, young girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she left me-- us with aunt. We cried and cried and cried. But we were alone now. Janina did have work.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, I was going to church for religious service. When I was coming out, the church was surrounded. There were Wehrmacht Nazis-- surrounded. They took all the young men and women. They let the older people and children go. They took us on the trucks. And they brought us to special encampment to be sent to Germany to work in the fields. Because they have the, the Jewish people-- they, you know, they brought them to Poland and this. But they needed-- in Germany-- workers to factories and fields. So we were in the encampment. Somehow, I was able, through the fence, to give people passing by to notify Helena Pavlovska, my, my aunt and my-- that I am here. And I remember Janina came, wanted visit me. Through the fence, she wanted to get in to me. She wanted to go with me. I pleaded with her. I say, at least-- I said, I don't know what will be with me, where I go. You have to go back. That night, I remember [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that some-- a few of these young Polish men, they tried to climb the fence and get out. They were shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now this was in Radom?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Radom, yes. And next day, I was prepared to leave. Instead that, about 10 German officers came. One of them was the major-- gentleman with the rank of major with the-- with the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] group of officers--

speaker: interviewer
text: Entourage.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and start picking at random. He picked about 20 people. And he picked me, too. We didn't know for what.

speaker: interviewee
text: They pack us on the-- on the trucks and brought us to ammunition factory. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So there I am. What I know about ammunition? They sent me to pack ammunition into little boxes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was weak, sick. I just came-- you know, I was anemic-- developed anemia. And I noticed there, for the first time again, I noticed was is happening in my country, how the SS is persecuting people, and what they doing to Jewish people. Because there again, there were Jewish people working there, too, in ammunition factory. And the men were taken. The women were treated badly. One day, the major came to inspect my station. I fainted. And I fainted right in front of him. And when I came to it, I sit on a chair. And he asked me my name. And then I told him Irene Gutovna. He say-- I say, my daddy is Gut. So he asked me if I am Volksdeutsche. I say, I am Polish. And I am Catholic. That was my answer.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, you are honest, too. You don't want to grab the opportunity to better yourself? I give you another job. So that's when he-- first time, you know, he gave me [NON-ENGLISH], you know, a paper, that I can go to my aunt's house. And that I have to show myself next day in this and this place or else. Before, where were you living?

speaker: interviewee
text: In, in the-- well, there was special encampment. I was with my aunt. And then when they captured me, I was in the special encampment. And now, when you are in the ammunition, I don't leave home. There was a barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: In this camp that you were in, were there only Poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish people were there, too. There were many women that were Jewish. But women was harder to find out. I didn't see men. But there were-- there were people, you know. Because from time to time, somebody grab somebody and this. But I, for the first time, just realized what is happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they treated the same way, though?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were-- maybe when they find them out, they were next to me. And next, next day, they are not there. I ask what it is. They are Jews, you know. And they were taken away. And there were different barracks, I guess. But anyway, the major now-- I promise I was [NON-ENGLISH]. So I came. My aunt and my sister were very happy. And they sent-- the next day I went to the work. And it was a big, huge hotel once in Radom. I have that on a picture even-- huge hotel, with the-- and then from that, the officers, major, his-- he did have workers-- officers and secretaries that lived there. And he gave me in care of Schultz. That was a not Gestapo. He was a Wehrmacht, short little guy with the red cheeks-- kind person, really. Because he said, you take care of her.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was so kind that he gave me special-- better to eat. He did the yellow eggs with wine and, and sugar to bring my blood. And, and he told me what to do. I was not-- I did cook. But I didn't have to serve, you know, tables, and this. And, and I did start watching-- the SS were running back and forth with their trucks. And what was happening, you know, from time to time through the window I did see. And I could not figure out why? What it is all about? Why? So one day, he told me that there would be a big party. And on the first floor, it's a ballroom and-- dining room and ballroom. So he wanted me to prepare the-- set the tables, clean the silver, and whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was doing that. Of a second, I heard such a noise-- shooting, yelling, screaming, people running, like, with pain. I pulled the drapes apart. And all I could see is SS running like wild ducks, shooting, killing, people running, children running. [CRYING] I mean, it's something that, even today, I can see it. I was standing, trembling, and paralyzed almost.

speaker: interviewee
text: Schultz walked in. And he-- and I said, Schultz, what it is? What's happening? What it is?

speaker: interviewee
text: He came and put his hand on my mouth. He said, [NON-ENGLISH]. The SS is coming right up. You don't want them to think you are a Jew-lover. It's the first time I really know what was happening. Now, I was working there. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the German now are not friends with the Russians, you know, anymore. They're now fighting the Russians. And Schultz said that soon, they will move. My sister doesn't want to stay alone with my-- with my aunt. And was-- her husband was killed in the war. And he's-- she-- so I said to-- she wanted to go with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she was very beautiful, tall, and voice like an angel, with the French hair, you know. She was gorgeous. Taller a whole head-- she was younger, but she was taller and developed nicely.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I told Schultz that-- because major said that I am going with him, that he's taking me. So I say that I have a sister. And I pleaded with him that, you know, if it-- and Schultz said, oh, we can always need another worker.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's how I was going with my sister. We were like gypsies. Not with major right away, but in the-- in the trucks behind. Where did they take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Lwow-- Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe what you saw when you looked out the window.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very sad, unbelievable. I did see blood running. I did see the screaming of children and the SS, just like wild dogs, just shooting. People were just scrambling to get out of the way. That's what I did see it. That was the first time. Now I am in Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you get to Lwow, did you find out why were they doing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Schultz put that hand on his mouth-- I should not be a Jew-lover. What that meant? I didn't have anyone to-- I could not ask Schultz. You see, I'm again alone. There is not anybody else. Now I am going to Lwow-- because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there in Lwow, we stop at a smaller place. I understood that they were-- major told the, the other-- the soldiers. Soldiers said that they are building big tank in Tarnopol. That is farther from Lwow. But they did not have ready the place. So now they are in Lwow. In Lwow, I met, accidentally, Helen, a Polish girl. We were in-- after church. I went to church. We were in park. And Helen-- we started speaking. And she trusted me. And she told me that her father was killed by the Germans, that a partisan did something. And they-- just because that's true, they go to 10, 15 houses and pull all the men and shot them. And that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Polish men? Jewish men?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish men now. Helen was Polish. But she's a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she said that, you know, her father was pulled with other men and killed because the partisans did something to the German. So they took it full out and were killing people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she see that? Was she there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she did see. The mother-- now, was-- she was only with mother together. And she did not-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she was very poor. She did not-- but she trusted me now and say that she is married to a Jewish man.
